en.extract:
The bell miner or bellbird (Manorina melanophrys) is a colonial honeyeater, endemic to southeastern Australia. The common name refers to their bell-like call. "Miner" is an old alternative spelling of "myna", and is shared with other members of the genus Manorina. The birds feed almost exclusively on the dome-like coverings, referred to as lerps, of certain psyllid bugs.
Bell miners live in large, complex, social groups. Within each group, there are subgroups consisting of several breeding pairs, but also including a number of birds that are not currently breeding. The nonbreeders help in providing food for the young in all the nests within the subgroup, even though they are not necessarily closely related to them. The birds defend their colony area communally and aggressively, excluding most other passerine species. They do this in order to protect their territory from other insect-eating birds that would eat the bell lerps on which they feed. Whenever the local forests die back, due to increased lerp psyllid infestations, bell miners undergo a population boom.
The heritage listed mountain village of Bellbrook was named after the distinctive sound of local bellbirds in 1882.


Taxonomy
The bell miner (Manorina melanophrys) belongs to the family of honeyeaters and Australian chats (Meliphagidae), which is part of the superfamily Meliphagoidea that also comprises the Australian warblers, scrubwrens, and thornbills (Acanthizidae); bristlebirds (Dasyornithidae); fairy-wrens (Maluridae); and pardalotes (Pardalotidae). Bell miners share the genus Manorina with three other endemic Australian miners: the noisy miner (M. melanocephala), the yellow-throated miner (M. flavigula), and the endangered black-eared miner (M. melanotis). The generic name Manorina is derived from the Ancient Greek manos 'thin' and rhinos 'nostrils'. The three other miners were previously classified in the genus Myzantha, which is still sometimes listed as a subgenus for those species. The closest related genus to Manorina has been genetically found to be the New Guinea and New Britain Melidectes honeyeaters. The bell miner's tinkling bell-like call was noted by early European explorers, and the name bellbird was considered established 30 years later, when David Collins mentioned "the melancholy cry of the bellbird". In 1802, John Latham named the bird Turdus melanophrys meaning 'black-browed thrush'. While Latham's scientific name became accepted, John Gould continued to use Australian bellbird as the name for the species in 1848.
The common name of bellbird may lead to some confusion with the Australian inland endemic crested bellbird, which is a member of the family Oreoicidae. The two species exhibit very different calls, behaviour, and do not overlap in range.


Description

Bell miners are the smallest of their genus, and differ from the other three predominantly grey miner species in having olive-green plumage, darker on the wings and yellower on the belly. They are a medium bodied honeyeater, slightly smaller and stockier than a Lewin's honeyeater (Meliphaga lewinii), weighing between 25 g and 35 g (average 29 g). Bell miners are 17.5–20 cm in length (average 18.5 cm) with a 22–30 cm wingspan (average 26.5 cm). They have the characteristic yellow bill of the miners, which is slightly downturned. The legs are bright orange, and the bare patch behind the eye is red-orange. The crown and lores are black, while the feathers in front of the eye are yellow. A dark streak runs from the corner of the bill downward giving a slight frowning appearance. Eyes are brown and mouth is yellow. Both sexes look alike, though the males tend to be slightly larger. It is possible to determine the sex of the birds by analyzing wing length, tail length, and culmen depth, or by observing calls that are unique to females, but there is no easy way to reliably determine the sex in the field without careful observation of behaviour and calls. Juveniles are more brown-colored than the adults and overall less bright in color. Young birds do not have the bare skin patch behind the eye. The patch initially develops as pale grey, then transforms to pale yellow, and darkens to pale orange before taking on the adult bright red-orange color as the bird matures. Nestlings are born naked and develop light brown down about two days post hatching.
The birds are heard more than they are seen, as bell miners tend to forage high in the canopy, and their olive-green plumage blends into the surrounding leaves. However, they keep up their "ping" contact call as they forage throughout the day. In the Royal Botanic Gardens, Melbourne, bell miners come low enough to be easily seen and photographed.


Distribution and habitat
Bell miners are distributed from around Gympie in Queensland, south along the coastal plain and ranges, to Victoria, around Melbourne. They prefer the margins of wet or dry sclerophyll forest and thick woodlands, often with a stream or other permanent water source nearby. This limits their range to higher rainfall areas near the coast, often bordering, but not inside, rainforest. Compared to the closely related noisy miner, bell miners prefer a denser habitat with a thick understory (<5 m), but a sparse midstory (5–15 m) and canopy (>15m). In an undisturbed setting, bell miners choose habitat with an understory of shrubs, ferns, sedges, and rainforest vines. They have been observed to expand their range in disturbed habitats that have a thick undergrowth of the invasive weed lantana. Bell miner population densities have been measured at 14 to 38 birds per hectare. They are particular about their preferred habitat and reasonably small disturbances to undergrowth, such as fire or lantana removal, can cause a colony to move to a new territory.


Behaviour


Social organization
The complex social organization of bell miners was observed as early as the 1960s in New South Wales, and has been studied by several research groups in Victoria. Bell miners live in large colonies of 8–200+ birds, which consist of coteries or clans of generally related male birds and their offspring. Each coterie is made up of several monogamous breeding pairs with their nest helpers. As a colony, bell miners are aggressive and set up a permanent territory that they will defend together against all other honeyeaters and any other species, which they perceive are a threat to their preferred food source or themselves. Within the colonial territory, each breeding pair has its own foraging range. Helpers assist multiple breeding pairs, and move between the foraging range of several pairs. Coteries are groups that are interconnected and interact daily within the larger colony, due to a close genetic relationship.
Due to their aggressive nature, bell miners are known for excluding other birds from their territory, and larger avian species, like kookaburras, currawongs, and ravens are mobbed by up to twelve miners from different coteries within the colony. Predators are repeatedly attacked, if they settle in another part of the colony's territory. Small birds that keep to the understory, like fairy-wrens, scrubwrens and blackbirds are often not driven out, but small birds that typically forage in the midstory or canopy or share similar foods, like pardalotes, are not allowed access within the territory. One of the few species that can sometimes displace bell miners is the similarly aggressive noisy miner, but in general noisy miners prefer areas with little understory. Bell miners are able to suppress the numbers of competing species in territory that they hold for years. However, bell miner colonies have clearly defined territorial boundaries, and beyond those boundaries the local bird assemblage resumes its normal diversity.


Feeding
Bell miners specialize in consuming insects known as psyllids and their associated young nymphs, sweet lerps, and other psyllid secretions. Psyllid products may consist of up to 90% of the bell miner's diet. Bell miners forage primarily among leaves, branches, and loose bark in the canopy, generally at least 8 m in height, but they do descend to the dense understory. There is a theory that bell miners 'farm' psyllids by excluding other psyllid-eating bird species from a large enough territory, that the miners themselves do not require all the psyllids from in order to sustain the colony. One hypothesis under the farming theory is that the bell miners may selectively eat only older nymphs, or may often eat the lerps and leave the nymph unharmed. Evidence for this theory has been mixed. An early study of stomach contents did not find supporting evidence for this theory, as bell miner stomachs did not contain the higher lerp/nymph ratio that would have been expected. However, a later behavioral comparison between bell miners and noisy miners did observe that bell miners carefully used their tongue to remove lerps, which left the nymph intact. In contrast, noisy miners pried the lerp and nymph off with their beak and consumed both. When bell miners are removed, psyllid colonies are generally quickly decimated by the other forest bird species that move into the miners' former territory.
Although psyllids are the primary food source, like most honeyeaters, bell miners have also been recorded drinking nectar from eucalyptus, banksia, and mistletoe flowers, as well as eating various other insects, including spiders, beetles, weevils, moths, and wasps.


Bell Miner Associated Dieback
Bell miners are so closely associated with eucalyptus dieback that the phenomenon has been named Bell Miner Associated Dieback (BMAD). Concern about BMAD has led to the formation of the BMAD Working Group, the 2004 BMAD Strategy, the 2005 BMAD National Forum, the 2006 BMAD Literature Review, and continues to be an area of active research. Eucalyptus dieback involved complicated ecosystems with numerous variables and occurs in some habitats without the presence of bell miners, but there is a high correlation with bell miner presence and eucalyptus dieback that was noted as early as 1982. One theory is that the monopolising and/or farming of psyllids by bell miners allows psyllid numbers to build up, eventually leading to tree sickness and possibly death. In some cases where bell miners have been removed, the avian diversity has been restored and the psyllid infestation reduced to the point of trees returning to health. However, in other studies, the trees did not recover even after bell miner removal, so more research is needed to better understand the relationship between bell miners and eucalyptus dieback.


Breeding

Bell miners are generally sedentary, and thus have a permanent all-purpose territory, which includes breeding. The primary breeding season is April/May to August/September in the northern part of their range and June/July to November/December in the southern range, but breeding has been observed in all months of the year, as females will often nest again after a failed nest or once the young have fledged. One female was recorded nesting five times in one season, though that many nest attempts is unusual. Bell miner pairs are monogamous, but the breeding tasks are divided between the sexes. Female bell miners build the nest over eight days, incubate the one to three (but typically two) eggs for 14.5 days, and brood the young for up to 12 days, depending on the weather. The nests are small, cup-shaped, and built with dry twigs, grasses, and bark woven together with spider web. Nests are generally hidden in the foliage of dense understory plants, 3–5 metres (9.8–16.4 ft) above the ground. The eggs are typically 24 by 16 millimetres (0.94 in × 0.63 in), oval shaped, and pink in color. They have darker reddish-brown spots and blotches, primarily over the larger end. Pallid cuckoos have been recorded as nest parasites for bell miners.
While nests are all within the territory of the colony, they are not tightly packed as traditional colony breeders. Instead, nests are located within the breeding pairs' normal foraging range. They are generally within calling range of neighbour nests, but not too close. Female bell miners move nest locations after a nest failure, and were observed to move nests lower into the thick understory, likely reducing the risk of avian predators. However, they did not move nests far from the original location horizontally, possibly to stay within their home foraging range, and to retain their established set of helpers. Females will also alter brood sex ratios to meet conditions. When a colony is in a new location with an inferior food source, the ratio will be biased towards more females, which will disperse from the colony at sexual maturity. Once the food supply has been increased, the brood sex ratio is shifted towards future helping males.


Nest helpers
Both parents and helpers provision and care for the nestlings. Nests may be attended by up to 20 helpers, and nest success has been observed to be higher when there are at least 6 additional helpers attending the nest, in addition to the breeding pair. Helpers feed the young, defend the nest against predators, remove droppings, and remove parasites. Nest helpers include both sexes, but are predominately young and breeding age males. Juvenile birds start helping as early as 1.3 months old. Older non-breeding males contribute the most food to nestlings and assist with multiple nests. The amount of help offered by a nest helper is correlated with its genetic relationship with the nestlings it is feeding. Bell miners use a provisioning call when they arrive at the nest to stimulate the young to beg. Provisioning calls are similar among closely related miners, so helpers preferentially help young with fathers that have provisioning calls similar to their own. However, it is important to note that even non-related helpers have been shown to contribute a significant amount to raising young, and no difference was seen in the amount of help offered by helpers, depending on the brood sex, even though male chicks tend to stay with the colony and become helpers, while females disperse. Helpers will delay bringing food to the nestlings when the female is brooding them, but will eventually bring food, even if the female continues to brood. The brooding female is more likely to allow the young to be fed if the attendant is her mate.


Fledgling to adulthood
Young bell miners leave the nest about 12 days post hatching, but continue to be fed by parents and helpers for a further 10 weeks. Bell miners will start helping at nests when less than 2 months old, but the transition is gradual, such that recently fledged birds will continue to beg from other helpers, and then either consume the offered food or give it to younger birds. Females tend to disperse from the colony at 8 months, and they reach breeding age at 8.3 months. It is likely that the mothers become intolerant of their daughters, as they reach maturity, prompting their dispersal. The mortality of bell miners prior to breeding age is very high at 93%. The greatest risk is soon after young bell miners leave the nest, when many are preyed upon. Known predators include grey currawong, Australian raven, laughing kookaburra, brown goshawk, copperhead snake, and eastern brown snake. By the time they reach breeding age, the sex ratio is skewed with more surviving males, probably due to higher mortality of the dispersing females.


References


External links

Bell Miner Associated Dieback
Photos, audio and video of bell miner from Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Recording of bell miner from Graeme Chapman's sound library

}}

The '''bell miner''' or '''bellbird''' ('''''Manorina melanophrys''''') is a colonial [[honeyeater]], [[Endemism|endemic]] to southeastern [[Australia]]. The common name refers to their bell-like call. "Miner" is an old alternative spelling of "[[myna]]", and is shared with other members of the genus ''[[Manorina]]''.<ref>For the derivation of "miner" from "myna", see comment at Common Indian Myna [http://fennerschool-associated.anu.edu.au/myna/] Retrieved 12 September 2013</ref> The birds feed almost exclusively on the dome-like coverings, referred to as [[Lerp (biology)|lerps]], of certain [[psyllid]] bugs.

Bell miners live in large, complex, social groups. Within each group, there are subgroups consisting of several breeding pairs, but also including a number of birds that are not currently breeding. The nonbreeders help in providing food for the young in all the nests within the subgroup, even though they are not necessarily closely related to them.<ref name="Ethology">{{cite journal | last1 = Clarke | first1 = M. F. | year = 1989 | title = The pattern of helping in the bell miner (''Manorina melanophrys'') | doi = 10.1111/j.1439-0310.1989.tb00748.x | journal = Ethology | volume = 80 | issue = 1–4| pages = 292–306 | bibcode = 1989Ethol..80..292C }}</ref> The birds defend their colony area communally and aggressively, excluding most other [[passerine]] species. They do this in order to protect their territory from other insect-eating birds that would eat the bell lerps on which they feed. Whenever the local forests die back, due to increased lerp psyllid infestations, bell miners undergo a population boom.

The heritage listed mountain village of [[Bellbrook, New South Wales|Bellbrook]] was named after the distinctive sound of local bellbirds in 1882.

==Taxonomy==
The bell miner (''Manorina melanophrys'') belongs to the family of [[honeyeater]]s and Australian chats ([[Meliphagidae]]), which is part of the superfamily [[Meliphagoidea]] that also comprises the Australian warblers, scrubwrens, and thornbills ([[Acanthizidae]]); bristlebirds ([[Dasyornithidae]]); fairy-wrens ([[Maluridae]]); and pardalotes ([[Pardalotidae]]).<ref name="Phylogeny">{{cite journal|last1=Gardner|first1=Janet L.|last2=Trueman|first2=John W.H.|last3=Ebert|first3=Daniel|last4=Joseph|first4=Leo|last5=Magrath|first5=Robert D.|title=Phylogeny and evolution of the Meliphagoidea, the largest radiation of Australasian songbirds|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|date=June 2010|volume=55|issue=3|pages=1087–1102|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2010.02.005|pmid=20152917 |bibcode=2010MolPE..55.1087G }}</ref> Bell miners share the genus ''[[Manorina]]'' with three other endemic Australian miners: the [[noisy miner]] (''M. melanocephala''), the [[yellow-throated miner]] (''M. flavigula''), and the endangered [[black-eared miner]] (''M. melanotis''). The generic name ''Manorina'' is derived from the Ancient Greek ''manos'' 'thin' and ''rhinos'' 'nostrils'.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird-names|last=Jobling|first=James A.|year=2010|access-date = 2020-04-25}}</ref> The three other miners were previously classified in the genus ''Myzantha'', which is still sometimes listed as a [[subgenus]] for those species. The closest related genus to ''[[Manorina]]'' has been genetically found to be the New Guinea and New Britain ''[[Melidectes]]'' honeyeaters. The bell miner's tinkling bell-like call was noted by early European explorers,<ref name="AustralianBirdNames">{{cite book|last1=Fraser|first1=Ian|last2=Gray|first2=Jeannie|title=Australian Bird Names: a Complete Guide|date=2013|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|location=Collingwood, Vic.|isbn=978-0-643-10469-3|page=204}}</ref> and the name bellbird was considered established 30 years later, when David Collins mentioned "the melancholy cry of the bellbird".<ref name="AustralianBirdNames" /><ref name="Collins">{{cite book|last1=Collins|first1=David|title=An Account of the English Colony of New South Wales Volume 2|date=1802|location=London|url=http://gutenberg.net.au/ebooks/e00011.html|access-date=21 March 2016}}</ref> In 1802, [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] named the bird ''Turdus melanophrys'' meaning 'black-browed thrush'.<ref name="AustralianBirdNames" /><ref name="Latham">{{cite book|last1=Latham|first1=J|title=Supplementum Indicis Ornithologici sive Systematis Ornithologiae|publisher=Leigh & Sotheby|date=1848|location=London|url=https://www.digitale-sammlungen.de/en/view/bsb10483009?page=1|access-date=21 March 2016}}</ref> While Latham's scientific name became accepted, John Gould continued to use Australian bellbird as the name for the species in 1848.<ref name="AustralianBirdNames" /><ref name="Gould">{{cite book|last1=Gould|first1=John|title=The Birds of Australia Volume 1-7|date=1848|location=London}}</ref>

The common name of bellbird may lead to some confusion with the Australian inland endemic [[crested bellbird]], which is a member of the family [[Oreoicidae]]. The two species exhibit very different calls, behaviour, and do not overlap in range.<ref name="Pizzey">{{cite book|last1=Pizzey|first1=Graham|last2=Knight|first2=Frank|title=The Field Guide to the Birds of Australia|edition=Ninth|date=2012|publisher=Harper Collins|location=Sydney|isbn=978-0-7322-9982-8}}</ref>

==Description==
[[Image:Bell Miner boulumba1.ogv|thumb|300px|left|Bouloumba Creek, SE Queensland, Australia]]

Bell miners are the smallest of their genus, and differ from the other three predominantly grey miner species in having olive-green [[plumage]], darker on the wings and yellower on the belly.<ref name="Officer">{{cite book |last1=Officer |first1=Hugh R. |title=Australian Honeyeaters |date=1971 |publisher=Bird Observers Club |others=[[Peg Maltby|Maltby, Peg]] (illustrator); Stirling, Alex (map) |isbn=978-0-909711-03-0 |edition=2nd |location=Melbourne |page=71}}</ref> They are a medium bodied honeyeater, slightly smaller and stockier than a [[Lewin's honeyeater]] (''Meliphaga lewinii''), weighing between 25 g and 35 g<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters">{{cite book|last1=Higgins|first1=P. J.|last2=Peter|first2=J. M.|last3=Steele|first3=W. K.|title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand & Antarctic Birds Volume 5:Tyrant Flycatchers to Chats|date=2001|publisher=Oxford Univ. Press|location=South Melbourne [u.a.]|isbn=978-0-19-553258-6|pages=609–626}}</ref> (average 29 g).<ref name="BirdInBackyards:BellMiner">{{cite web|title=Bell Miner|url=http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Manorina-melanophrys|website=Birds In Backyards|publisher=Birdlife Australia|access-date=18 March 2016}}</ref> Bell miners are 17.5–20 cm in length (average 18.5 cm) with a 22–30 cm wingspan (average 26.5 cm).<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> They have the characteristic yellow bill of the miners, which is slightly downturned. The legs are bright orange, and the bare patch behind the eye is red-orange.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> The crown and lores are black, while the feathers in front of the eye are yellow. A dark streak runs from the corner of the bill downward<ref name="Officer" /> giving a slight frowning appearance. Eyes are brown and mouth is yellow.<ref name="BandersAid">{{cite book|last1=Ken|first1=Rogers|last2=Rogers|first2=Annie|last3=Rogers|first3=Danny|title=Bander's Aid: A Guide to Ageing and Sexing Bush Birds|date=1986|publisher=A. Rogers|location=St. Andrews, Vic.|isbn=978-1-86252-640-2|pages=86–87|url=http://birdlife.org.au/documents/OTHPUB-Banders-Aid.pdf|access-date=18 March 2016|archive-date=19 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221019222709/https://birdlife.org.au/documents/OTHPUB-Banders-Aid.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> Both sexes look alike, though the males tend to be slightly larger.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> It is possible to determine the sex of the birds by analyzing wing length, tail length, and [[Culmen (beak)|culmen]] depth, or by observing calls that are unique to females, but there is no easy way to reliably determine the sex in the field without careful observation of behaviour and calls.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Clarke|first1=MF|last2=Heathcote|first2=CF|title=Methods for sexing and aging the Bell Miner ''Manorina melanophrys'' |journal=Emu|date=1988|volume=88|issue=2|page=118|doi=10.1071/MU9880118|bibcode=1988EmuAO..88..118C }}</ref> Juveniles are more brown-colored than the adults and overall less bright in color. Young birds do not have the bare skin patch behind the eye. The patch initially develops as pale grey, then transforms to pale yellow, and darkens to pale orange before taking on the adult bright red-orange color as the bird matures.<ref name="BandersAid" /><ref name="ABSA">{{cite book|last1=Hardy|first1=J. W.|title=Bird in the Hand|edition=2nd|url=http://www.absa.asn.au/absainc/wp-content/uploads/2015/03/Bell-Miner.pdf|access-date=4 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160320035624/http://www.absa.asn.au/absainc/wp-content/uploads/2015/03/Bell-Miner.pdf|archive-date=20 March 2016}}</ref> Nestlings are born naked and develop light brown down about two days post hatching.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" />

The birds are heard more than they are seen, as bell miners tend to forage high in the [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]], and their olive-green plumage blends into the surrounding leaves. However, they keep up their "ping" contact call as they forage throughout the day.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> In the [[Royal Botanic Gardens, Melbourne]], bell miners come low enough to be easily seen and photographed.

==Distribution and habitat==
Bell miners are distributed from around [[Gympie]] in [[Queensland]], south along the coastal plain and ranges, to [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]], around [[Melbourne]].<ref name="Officer" /><ref name="HoneyeaterDistribution">{{cite journal|last1=Gannon|first1=G.R.|title=Distribution of the Australian honeyeaters |journal=Emu|date=1962|volume=62|issue=3|pages=145–166|doi=10.1071/MU962145|bibcode=1962EmuAO..62..145G }}</ref> They prefer the margins of wet or dry [[sclerophyll]] forest and thick woodlands, often with a stream or other permanent water source nearby.<ref name="NestEgg">{{cite book|last1=Beruldsen|first1=Gordon|title=Australian Birds their Nests and Eggs|date=2003|publisher=G. Beruldsen|location=Kenmore Hills, Qld.|isbn=978-0-646-42798-0|page=313|edition=rev.}}</ref> This limits their range to higher rainfall areas near the coast, often bordering, but not inside, rainforest. Compared to the closely related noisy miner,<ref name ="Birdlife">{{cite book|last1=Rowley|first1=Ian|title=Bird Life|date=1974|publisher=Collins|location=Sydney|isbn=978-0-00-211438-7|pages=119–120}}</ref> bell miners prefer a denser habitat with a thick [[understory]] (<5 m), but a sparse midstory (5–15 m) and canopy (>15m).<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Lambert|first1=Kathryn T. A.|last2=Kumar|first2=Lalit|last3=Reid|first3=Nick|last4=McDonald|first4=Paul G.|title=Habitat selection by a despotic passerine, the Bell Miner (''Manorina melanophrys''): When restoring habitat through Lantana (''Lantana camara'') removal is not enough |journal=Ecological Management & Restoration|date=January 2016|volume=17|issue=1|pages=81–84|doi=10.1111/emr.12196|bibcode=2016EcoMR..17...81L |doi-access=free}}</ref> In an undisturbed setting, bell miners choose habitat with an understory of shrubs, ferns, sedges, and rainforest vines.<ref name="Thesis">{{cite thesis |last=Hastings |first=Tony |date=May 2012 |title=Rainforests and Bell Miner Associated Dieback in Natural Ecosystems |url=http://www.tonyhastings.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2015/reports/hastings%20BMAD%20thesis.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160418194635/http://www.tonyhastings.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2015/reports/hastings%20BMAD%20thesis.pdf |archive-date=18 April 2016 |type=B. Env. Sc. Honours |location=Wagga Wagga, NSW |publisher=Charles Sturt University |page=85 |access-date=21 March 2016}}</ref> They have been observed to expand their range in disturbed habitats that have a thick undergrowth of the invasive weed [[lantana]].<ref name="LantanaMiners">{{cite journal|last1=Somerville|first1=Susan|last2=Somerville|first2=Wayne|last3=Coyle|first3=Rodney|title=Regenerating native forest using splatter gun techniques to remove Lantana|journal=Ecological Management & Restoration|date=December 2011|volume=12|issue=3|pages=164–174|doi=10.1111/j.1442-8903.2011.00608.x|bibcode=2011EcoMR..12..164S }}</ref> Bell miner population densities have been measured at 14 to 38 birds per hectare.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Kemmerer|first1=Ernst P.|last2=Shields|first2=Jim M.|last3=Tidemann|first3=Christopher R.|title=High densities of bell miners ''Manorina melanophrys'' associated with reduced diversity of other birds in wet eucalypt forest: Potential for adaptive management|journal=Forest Ecology and Management|date=April 2008|volume=255|issue=7|pages=2094–2102|doi=10.1016/j.foreco.2007.12.035 |bibcode=2008ForEM.255.2094K }}</ref> They are particular about their preferred habitat and reasonably small disturbances to undergrowth, such as fire or lantana removal, can cause a colony to move to a new territory.<ref name="NestEgg" />

==Behaviour==
===Social organization===
The complex social organization of bell miners was observed as early as the 1960s<ref name="HoneyeaterDistribution" /> in [[New South Wales]], and has been studied by several research groups in [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]].<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> Bell miners live in large colonies of 8–200+ birds,<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Hyem|first1=E.L|title=Notes on the Birds of 'Mernot,' Barrington, N.S.W.|journal=Emu|date=1936|volume=36|issue=4|pages=262–272|doi=10.1071/MU936262}}</ref> which consist of coteries or clans of generally related male birds and their offspring. Each coterie is made up of several monogamous breeding pairs with their nest helpers.<ref name="GeneticStructure">{{cite journal|last1=Painter|first1=J. N.|last2=Crozier|first2=R. H.|last3=Poiani|first3=A.|last4=Robertson|first4=R. J.|last5=Clarke|first5=M. F.|title=Complex social organization reflects genetic structure and relatedness in the cooperatively breeding bell miner, ''Manorina melanophrys''|journal=Molecular Ecology|date=September 2000|volume=9|issue=9|pages=1339–1347|doi=10.1046/j.1365-294x.2000.01012.x|pmid=10972773|bibcode=2000MolEc...9.1339P |s2cid=26273073}}</ref> As a colony, bell miners are aggressive and set up a permanent territory that they will defend together against all other honeyeaters and any other species, which they perceive are a threat to their preferred food source or themselves.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> Within the colonial territory, each [[breeding pair]] has its own foraging range. Helpers assist multiple breeding pairs, and move between the foraging range of several pairs. Coteries are groups that are interconnected and interact daily within the larger colony, due to a close genetic relationship.<ref name="KinSelection">{{cite journal|last1=Clarke|first1=Michael F.|title=Co-operative breeding by the Australian Bell Miner ''Manorina melanophrys'' Latham: a test of kin selection theory |journal=Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology|date=1984|volume=14|issue=2|pages=137–146|jstor=4599660|doi=10.1007/bf00291904|bibcode=1984BEcoS..14..137C |s2cid=33874861}}</ref>

Due to their aggressive nature, bell miners are known for excluding other birds from their territory, and larger avian species, like kookaburras, currawongs, and ravens are mobbed by up to twelve miners from different coteries within the colony. Predators are repeatedly attacked, if they settle in another part of the colony's territory. Small birds that keep to the understory, like fairy-wrens, scrubwrens and blackbirds are often not driven out,<ref name="SocialOrganization">{{cite journal|last1=Smith|first1=AJ|last2=Robertson|first2=BI|title=Social Organization of Bell Miners|journal=Emu|date=1978|volume=78|issue=4|page=169|doi=10.1071/MU9780169|bibcode=1978EmuAO..78..169S }}</ref> but small birds that typically forage in the midstory or canopy or share similar foods, like pardalotes, are not allowed access within the territory. One of the few species that can sometimes displace bell miners is the similarly aggressive noisy miner, but in general noisy miners prefer areas with little understory.<ref name="InterspeciesAggression">{{cite journal|last1=Clarke|first1=MF|title=Interspecific Aggression Within the Genus ''Manorina''|journal=Emu|date=1984|volume=84|issue=2|pages=113–115|doi=10.1071/MU9840113 |bibcode=1984EmuAO..84..113C }}</ref> Bell miners are able to suppress the numbers of competing species in territory that they hold for years.<ref name="AsymmetricalCompetition">{{cite journal|last1=Poiani|first1=A.|last2=Rogers|first2=A.|last3=Rogers|first3=K.|last4=Rogers|first4=D.|title=Asymmetrical competition between the bell miner (''Manorina melanophrys'', Meliphagidae) and other honeyeaters: evidence from Southeastern Victoria, Australia|journal=Oecologia|date=December 1990|volume=85|issue=2|pages=250–256|doi=10.1007/BF00319410|pmid=28312564|bibcode=1990Oecol..85..250P |s2cid=25891981}}</ref> However, bell miner colonies have clearly defined territorial boundaries, and beyond those boundaries the local bird assemblage resumes its normal diversity.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Leseberg|first1=Nicholas P.|last2=Lambert|first2=Kathryn T. A.|last3=McDonald|first3=Paul G.|title=Fine-scale impacts on avian biodiversity due to a despotic species, the bell miner (''Manorina melanophrys'')|journal=Austral Ecology|date=May 2015|volume=40|issue=3|pages=245–254|doi=10.1111/aec.12206 |bibcode=2015AusEc..40..245L }}</ref>

===Feeding===
Bell miners specialize in consuming insects known as [[Jumping plant louse|psyllid]]s and their associated young nymphs, sweet [[lerp (biology)|lerps]], and other psyllid secretions. Psyllid products may consist of up to 90% of the bell miner's diet.<ref name="LoynScience">{{cite journal|last1=Loyn|first1=Richard H.|title=Territorial Bell Miners and Other Birds Affecting Populations of Insect Prey|journal=Science|date=September 30, 1983|volume=221|issue=4618|pages=1411–3|doi=10.1126/science.221.4618.1411|pmid=17759017|bibcode=1983Sci...221.1411L |s2cid=37621159}}</ref> Bell miners forage primarily among leaves, branches, and loose bark in the canopy, generally at least 8 m in height, but they do descend to the dense understory.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> There is a theory that bell miners 'farm' psyllids by excluding other psyllid-eating bird species from a large enough territory, that the miners themselves do not require all the psyllids from in order to sustain the colony.<ref name="FarmsDell">{{cite journal|last1=Loyn|first1=Richard H.|title=The Bird that Farms the Dell|journal=Natural History (Pre-1988)|date=June 1987|volume=96|issue=6}}</ref> One hypothesis under the farming theory is that the bell miners may selectively eat only older nymphs, or may often eat the lerps and leave the nymph unharmed.<ref name="FarmsDell" /> Evidence for this theory has been mixed. An early study of stomach contents did not find supporting evidence for this theory, as bell miner stomachs did not contain the higher lerp/nymph ratio that would have been expected.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Poiani|first1=A|title=Bell Miners: What Kind of Farmers Are They?|journal=Emu|date=1993|volume=93|issue=3|pages=188–194|doi=10.1071/MU9930188|bibcode=1993EmuAO..93..188P}}</ref> However, a later behavioral comparison between bell miners and noisy miners did observe that bell miners carefully used their tongue to remove lerps, which left the nymph intact. In contrast, noisy miners pried the lerp and nymph off with their beak and consumed both.<ref name="NonLethalForage">{{cite journal|last1=Haythorpe|first1=Kathryn M.|last2=McDonald|first2=Paul G.|title=Non-lethal foraging by bell miners on a herbivorous insect: Potential implications for forest health|journal=Austral Ecology|date=25 February 2010|volume=35|issue=4|pages=444–450|doi=10.1111/j.1442-9993.2009.02099.x|bibcode=2010AusEc..35..444H }}</ref> When bell miners are removed, psyllid colonies are generally quickly decimated by the other forest bird species that move into the miners' former territory.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /><ref name="LoynScience" />

Although psyllids are the primary food source, like most honeyeaters, bell miners have also been recorded drinking nectar from eucalyptus, banksia, and mistletoe flowers, as well as eating various other insects, including spiders, beetles, weevils, moths, and wasps.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /><ref name="Food">{{cite book|last1=Barker|first1=R.D.|last2=Vestjens|first2=W.J.M.|title=The Food of Australian Birds|date=1989|publisher=CSIRO Division of Wildlife and Ecology|location=Lyneham, A.C.T.|isbn=978-0-643-05115-7|pages=196–197}}</ref>

====Bell Miner Associated Dieback====
Bell miners are so closely associated with eucalyptus dieback that the phenomenon has been named Bell Miner Associated Dieback (BMAD). Concern about BMAD has led to the formation of the BMAD Working Group,<ref name="WorkingGroup">{{cite web|title=Bell Miner Associated Dieback Working Group|url=http://www.bmad.com.au/workinggroup.php|website=Bell Miner Associated Dieback|access-date=20 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160227040310/http://bmad.com.au/workinggroup.php|archive-date=27 February 2016}}</ref> the 2004 BMAD Strategy,<ref name="Strategy">{{cite book|title=Bell Miner Associated Dieback Strategy 2004|date=2004|publisher=Bell Miner Associated Dieback Working Group|pages=1–19|url=http://www.bmad.com.au/publications/BMAD_Strategy_2004.pdf|access-date=20 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160312235403/http://www.bmad.com.au/publications/BMAD_Strategy_2004.pdf|archive-date=12 March 2016}}</ref> the 2005 BMAD National Forum,<ref name="NationalForum">{{cite book|title=Proceedings of the Bell Miner Associated Dieback National Forum|date=March 30, 2005|location=Lismore, NSW, Australia |publisher=Southern Cross University |url=http://www.bmad.com.au/publications/BMAD_Proceedings.pdf|access-date=20 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160312174358/http://www.bmad.com.au/publications/BMAD_Proceedings.pdf|archive-date=12 March 2016}}</ref> the 2006 BMAD Literature Review,<ref name="LitReview">{{cite book|last1=Wardell-Johnson|first1=Grant|last2=Stone|first2=Christine|last3=Recher|first3=Harry|last4=Lynch|first4=A. Jasmyn J.|title=Bell Miner Associated Dieback (BMAD) Independent Scientific Literature Review: A Review of Eucalypt Dieback Associated with Bell Miner Habitat in North-eastern New South Wales, Australia|date=2006|publisher=Dept. of Environment and Conservation (NSW)|location=[Coffs Harbour, N.S.W.]|isbn=978-1-74137-856-6|url=http://www.bmad.com.au/publications/LiteratureReview.pdf|access-date=20 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160227040451/http://bmad.com.au/publications/LiteratureReview.pdf|archive-date=27 February 2016}}</ref> and continues to be an area of active research. Eucalyptus dieback involved complicated ecosystems with numerous variables and occurs in some habitats without the presence of bell miners,<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Jurskis|first1=Vic|title=Eucalypt decline in Australia, and a general concept of tree decline and dieback|journal=Forest Ecology and Management|date=August 2005|volume=215|issue=1–3|pages=1–20|doi=10.1016/j.foreco.2005.04.026 |bibcode=2005ForEM.215....1J }}</ref> but there is a high correlation with bell miner presence and eucalyptus dieback that was noted as early as 1982. One theory is that the monopolising and/or farming of psyllids by bell miners allows psyllid numbers to build up, eventually leading to tree sickness and possibly death.<ref name="SurveyCrownDieback">{{cite book|last1=Stone|first1=C.|last2=Spolc|first2=D.|last3=Urquhart|first3=C.A.|title=Survey of Crown Dieback in Moist Hardwood Forests in the Central and Northern Regions of New South Wales State Forests (Psyllid/bell miner Research Programme)|date=1995|publisher=Research Division, State Forests of New South Wales|location=Sydney|isbn=978-0-7310-2216-8}}</ref> In some cases where bell miners have been removed, the avian diversity has been restored and the psyllid infestation reduced to the point of trees returning to health.<ref name="LoynScience" /> However, in other studies, the trees did not recover even after bell miner removal, so more research is needed to better understand the relationship between bell miners and eucalyptus dieback.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Clarke|first1=Michael F|last2=Schedvin|first2=Natasha|title=Removal of bell miners ''Manorina melanophrys'' from ''Eucalyptus radiata'' forest and its effect on avian diversity, psyllids and tree health|journal=Biological Conservation|date=April 1999|volume=88|issue=1|pages=111–120|doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(98)00083-4|bibcode=1999BCons..88..111C }}</ref>

===Breeding===
[[File:BabyBellMiner1.jpg|thumb|right|Immature birds]]

Bell miners are generally sedentary, and thus have a permanent all-purpose territory, which includes breeding. The primary breeding season is April/May to August/September in the northern part of their range and June/July to November/December in the southern range,<ref name="NestEgg" /> but breeding has been observed in all months of the year,<ref name="SocialOrganization" /><ref name="ReproductiveBehavior">{{cite journal|last1=Clarke|first1=MF|title=The Reproductive Behavior of the Bell Miner|journal=Emu|date=1988|volume=88|issue=2|pages=88–100|doi=10.1071/MU9880088}}</ref> as females will often nest again after a failed nest or once the young have fledged. One female was recorded nesting five times in one season, though that many nest attempts is unusual.<ref name="SocialOrganization" /> Bell miner pairs are monogamous, but the breeding tasks are divided between the sexes. Female bell miners build the nest over eight days, incubate the one to three (but typically two) eggs for 14.5 days, and brood the young for up to 12 days, depending on the weather.<ref name="ReproductiveBehavior" /> The nests are small, cup-shaped, and built with dry twigs, grasses, and bark woven together with spider web.<ref name="NestEgg" /> Nests are generally hidden in the foliage of dense understory plants, {{Convert|3|-|5|m|ft}} above the ground.<ref name="NestEgg" /> The eggs are typically {{Convert|24|x|16|mm}}, oval shaped, and pink in color. They have darker reddish-brown spots and blotches, primarily over the larger end.<ref name="NestEgg" /> [[Pallid cuckoo|Pallid cuckoos]] have been recorded as nest parasites for bell miners.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Swainson|first1=G.W.|title=Co-operative rearing in the Bell Miner|journal=Emu|date=1970|volume=70|issue=4|pages=183–188|doi=10.1071/MU970183|bibcode=1970EmuAO..70..183S }}</ref>

While nests are all within the territory of the colony, they are not tightly packed as traditional colony breeders. Instead, nests are located within the breeding pairs' normal foraging range. They are generally within calling range of neighbour nests, but not too close.<ref name="SpacialOrg">{{cite journal|last1=Clarke|first1=MF|last2=Fitzgerald|first2=GF|title=Spatial-Organization of the Cooperatively Breeding Bell Miner|journal=Emu|date=1994|volume=94|issue=2|page=96|doi=10.1071/MU9940096}}</ref> Female bell miners move nest locations after a nest failure, and were observed to move nests lower into the thick understory, likely reducing the risk of avian predators. However, they did not move nests far from the original location horizontally, possibly to stay within their home foraging range, and to retain their established set of helpers.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Beckmann|first1=Christa|last2=McDonald|first2=Paul G.|title=Placement of Re-nests Following Predation: Are Birds Managing Risk?|journal=Emu|date=2016|volume=116|issue=1 |pages=9–13|doi=10.1071/mu15064|bibcode=2016EmuAO.116....9B |s2cid=88358633}}</ref> Females will also alter brood [[Human sex ratio|sex ratios]] to meet conditions. When a colony is in a new location with an inferior food source, the ratio will be biased towards more females, which will disperse from the colony at sexual maturity. Once the food supply has been increased, the brood sex ratio is shifted towards future helping males.<ref name="BroodSexRatio">{{cite journal|last1=Ewen|first1=J. G.|title=Facultative control of offspring sex in the cooperatively breeding bell miner, ''Manorina melanophrys''|journal=Behavioral Ecology|date=1 March 2003|volume=14|issue=2|pages=157–164|doi=10.1093/beheco/14.2.157|doi-access=free}}</ref>

====Nest helpers====
Both parents and helpers provision and care for the nestlings. Nests may be attended by up to 20 helpers,<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> and nest success has been observed to be higher when there are at least 6 additional helpers attending the nest, in addition to the breeding pair.<ref name="Ethology" /> Helpers feed the young, defend the nest against predators, remove droppings, and remove parasites.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> Nest helpers include both sexes, but are predominately young and breeding age males. Juvenile birds start helping as early as 1.3 months old.<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> Older non-breeding males contribute the most food to nestlings and assist with multiple nests.<ref name="Ethology" /> The amount of help offered by a nest helper is correlated with its genetic relationship with the nestlings it is feeding.<ref name="KinSelection" /> Bell miners use a provisioning call when they arrive at the nest to stimulate the young to beg. Provisioning calls are similar among closely related miners, so helpers preferentially help young with fathers that have provisioning calls similar to their own.<ref name="BeggingCalls">{{cite journal|last1=McDonald|first1=P. G.|last2=Wright|first2=J.|title=Bell miner provisioning calls are more similar among relatives and are used by helpers at the nest to bias their effort towards kin|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|date=30 March 2011|volume=278|issue=1723|pages=3403–3411|doi=10.1098/rspb.2011.0307|pmid=21450738|pmc=3177633 |bibcode=2011PBioS.278.3403M }}</ref> However, it is important to note that even non-related helpers have been shown to contribute a significant amount to raising young,<ref name="NonRelatedHelpers">{{cite journal|last1=Wright|first1=J.|last2=McDonald|first2=P. G.|last3=te Marvelde|first3=L.|last4=Kazem|first4=A. J. N.|last5=Bishop|first5=C. M.|title=Helping effort increases with relatedness in bell miners, but 'unrelated' helpers of both sexes still provide substantial care|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|date=21 October 2009|volume=277|issue=1680|pages=437–445|doi=10.1098/rspb.2009.1360|pmid=19846458|pmc=2842640}}</ref> and no difference was seen in the amount of help offered by helpers, depending on the brood sex, even though male chicks tend to stay with the colony and become helpers, while females disperse.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=McDonald|first1=Paul G.|last2=Ewen|first2=John G.|last3=Wright|first3=Jonathan|title=Brood sex ratio does not affect helper effort in a cooperative bird, despite extreme sex-biased dispersal|journal=Animal Behaviour|date=January 2010|volume=79|issue=1|pages=243–250|doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2009.11.007|bibcode=2010AnBeh..79..243M |s2cid=53160622}}</ref> Helpers will delay bringing food to the nestlings when the female is brooding them, but will eventually bring food, even if the female continues to brood. The brooding female is more likely to allow the young to be fed if the attendant is her mate.<ref name="BroodingBehavior">{{cite journal|last1=Archard|first1=G.A.|last2=Robertson|first2=R.J.|last3=Jones|first3=D.|last4=Painter|first4=J.|last5=Crozier|first5=R.|last6=Clarke|first6=M.F.|title=Brooding behaviour in the cooperatively breeding Bell Miner (''Manorina melanophrys'')|journal=Emu|date=1 January 2006|volume=106|issue=2|pages=105–112|doi=10.1071/mu05032|bibcode=2006EmuAO.106..105A |s2cid=84151250}}</ref>

====Fledgling to adulthood====
Young bell miners leave the nest about 12 days post hatching, but continue to be fed by parents and helpers for a further 10 weeks.<ref name="ReproductiveBehavior" /> Bell miners will start helping at nests when less than 2 months old, but the transition is gradual, such that recently fledged birds will continue to beg from other helpers, and then either consume the offered food or give it to younger birds.<ref name="KinSelection" /> Females tend to disperse from the colony at 8 months, and they reach breeding age at 8.3 months. It is likely that the mothers become intolerant of their daughters, as they reach maturity, prompting their dispersal. The mortality of bell miners prior to breeding age is very high at 93%.<ref name="Dispersal">{{cite journal|last1=Clarke|first1=MF|last2=Heathcote|first2=CF|title=Dispersal, Survivorship and Demography in the Co-Operatively-Breeding Bell Miner|journal=Emu|date=1990|volume=90|issue=1|page=15|doi=10.1071/MU9900015}}</ref> The greatest risk is soon after young bell miners leave the nest, when many are preyed upon.<ref name="SocialOrganization" /> Known predators include [[grey currawong]], [[Australian raven]], [[laughing kookaburra]], [[brown goshawk]], [[copperhead snake]],{{Disambiguation needed|date=May 2026}} and [[eastern brown snake]].<ref name="HANZAB:Honeyeaters" /> By the time they reach breeding age, the sex ratio is skewed with more surviving males, probably due to higher mortality of the dispersing females.<ref name="Dispersal" />
